planting.note: Root cuttings in spring or start from seed; Greek sage is more heat and drought tolerant than common sage (Salvia officinalis) and thrives in the alkaline, rocky, dry conditions of Mediterranean-type climates and zone 8–10 gardens; excellent in raised beds with sharp drainage.

directions: Root stem cuttings or sow seeds in well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Greek sage (Salvia fruticosa) is the wild sage of the eastern Mediterranean islands — the primary sage of Greek, Cypriot, and Turkish cuisine and the sage used in dried herb mixes sold as 'mountain tea' throughout Greece and the Middle East. The flavor is fuller, more complex, and somewhat milder than common garden sage, with distinctive 3-lobed leaves (giving it the name 'three-lobed sage') and lavender-pink flowers. More heat tolerant and longer-lived than common sage in hot-summer climates, making it an excellent culinary sage for zones 8–11 where Salvia officinalis tends to decline. Used fresh or dried in Greek lamb dishes, lemon-sage teas, and Mediterranean marinades.

planting.note: Divide in spring or fall; hairy mountain mint self-seeds moderately and can naturalize in meadows and garden beds — a valuable addition to pollinator gardens that spreads slowly to form attractive colonies. Performs well in average to dry garden soils.

directions: Plant divisions or transplants in full sun to light shade in average, dry to moist, well-drained soil; hairy mountain mint (Pycnanthemum pilosum) is a native prairie and woodland-edge mint of the central and eastern United States with strongly aromatic foliage — the intense spearmint-oregano fragrance is released when leaves are brushed — and masses of small white flowers with purple spots that are exceptionally attractive to native bees, butterflies, and wasps from July through September, making it one of the highest-rated native plants for pollinator gardens. The dried leaves can be used as a mild mint tea or herb. Unlike invasive common mint (Mentha), mountain mints spread slowly and politely. An outstanding native herb for the meadow garden, pollinator strip, and naturalizing in dry to average soils.

planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings floated in water or planted in wet soil; water mimosa requires warm, shallow, still or slow-moving water or consistently wet mud in full sun. Zone 9-12 only; potentially invasive in Florida and Hawaii where it escapes waterways.

directions: Root stem cuttings in shallow water or wet mud in full sun; water mimosa (Neptunia oleracea) is an aquatic legume of tropical Asia, Africa, and the Americas, floating on the surface of ponds, ditches, and slow-moving water with spongy, air-filled white stems that provide buoyancy. The young shoots, leaves, and flower buds are widely eaten as a vegetable in Thai, Vietnamese, and Southeast Asian cuisine — blanched or stir-fried with a mild, crisp texture and slightly mucilaginous character similar to water spinach. The leaves also fold up when touched, like the related sensitive plant (Mimosa pudica). Caution: it is listed as invasive in Florida and should be grown only in contained water features in that state; elsewhere it is an interesting aquatic edible for warm-climate water gardens.

planting.note: Divide or transplant in spring or fall; Braun's holly fern prefers cool, moist, shaded positions with rich, well-drained, slightly acidic humus soil — it is more demanding than Christmas fern and best suited to consistently cool, moist woodland gardens.

directions: Plant in shade in consistently moist, rich, well-drained, slightly acidic humus soil; Braun's holly fern (Polystichum braunii) is a graceful, evergreen to semi-evergreen woodland fern with lustrous, dark green, double-pinnate fronds covered in silvery scales when young — one of the most attractive of all hardy ferns when grown in ideal conditions. Native to cool, moist forest understories of the northern United States, Europe, and northern Asia, it is best adapted to consistently cool and moist woodland positions and is not suitable for hot, dry summers. An outstanding choice for shade gardens in zones 3–7 providing year-round evergreen texture; it pairs beautifully with trilliums, Solomon's seal, and other woodland natives. More refined and delicate in appearance than Christmas fern or marginal shield fern.
